sound_type: amplified chorus in wet forest -- multiple males singing simultaneously in rain, 113+ notes per song, E=5, wet old-growth acoustic pocket
ecosystem: Wet old-growth -- Douglas-fir, western red cedar, Sitka spruce with dense fern understory; forest floor log tangles and root masses
season: Storm day (Pacific Wrens sing MORE actively in rain; peak chorus on rainy storm days)
milestone: BIOLOGY RETURNS -- 23 consecutive abiotic degrees (245-267) end. Last biological entry was White Sturgeon at degree 244. Pacific Wren rain-day chorus is the first biological re-entry.

coordinates.description: Reference for wet old-growth Pacific Wren habitat in the Cascades and Olympics. Troglodytes pacificus distributed throughout wet forest of the PNW coast and Cascades.

taxonomy_note.description: RETURN from degree 001. Pacific Wren was the FIRST species entry in the entire SPS catalog. Returns at degree 268 in rain-day chorus context. 267-degree gap. Degree 001 documented the general Pacific Wren; degree 268 isolates the rain-day chorus -- the behavioral context that makes the species extraordinary in the PNW. 2010 species split from Winter Wren (Troglodytes hiemalis). Song complexity differences were among the evidence for the split.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Troglodytidae entries: Pacific Wren (001, 268), Bewick's Wren (055)
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Troglodytes from Greek trogle (hole, cave) + dyein (to dive into) = cave-dweller. pacificus = of the Pacific. The Pacific cave-dweller -- foraging in dark tangles of the forest floor.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Troglodytes: the wren genus. Small passerines defined by song complexity and habit of disappearing into dense vegetation. Short cocked tail is the family visual signature.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Passeriformes (perching birds, songbirds). First Passeriformes since the fish-to-geophysics arc. Biology returns with the songbirds.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Second Troglodytidae entry. Bewick's Wren was first (degree 055, shrubby edge habitat). Pacific Wren in wet old-growth interior represents the family's deepest forest habitat range.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 2,500-9,000+ Hz. Wide spectral range. Penetrates forest ambient noise across a broad frequency band.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 3,000-6,000 Hz. The core of the song energy, with harmonics extending to 9,000+ Hz.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Continuous cascade from ~2,500 Hz to 9,000+ Hz. No single fundamental -- the song is a rapid sequence of note types each with its own frequency character.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: 5-10 seconds per continuous song bout. Multiple bouts per minute during active singing. On storm days, near-continuous singing for hours.
song_metrics.note_structure: 113+ DISTINCT NOTE TYPES per continuous song bout. ~16 notes per second at peak delivery. Notes are distinct under acoustic slow-motion analysis; blur into continuous cascading trill in real time. No simple repeated phrases -- constant variation throughout.
song_metrics.note_types: 113+ distinct note types. Individual males have repertoires of 20-40+ song types rotated through a singing bout. No two neighboring males sing exactly the same song. Complexity is both genetic (capacity) and learned (specific note types from adults in the neighborhood).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Continuous cascade with no simple repeated loop. Unlike other songbirds that repeat phrases, the Pacific Wren delivers a near-continuous stream of varying notes. The song has structure -- but the structure is complex, not repetitive.
song_metrics.timbre: Rich, penetrating, cascading. The wide frequency range gives the song its characteristic 'spilling over itself' quality. In wet old-growth, the song seems to fill all acoustic space simultaneously.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=5 -- engaged, detailed, present. Small bird (8-12 grams) at full effort. Not overwhelming in volume but overwhelming in information density. The most note-dense E=5 in the engine.
song_metrics.tempo: ~16 notes per second at peak. Faster than human speech. The fastest note-delivery rate of any North American songbird.
song_metrics.context: TERRITORIAL AND MATE ATTRACTION. Males sing to establish territory and attract females. Song complexity honestly signals cognitive and neurological quality (sexual selection hypothesis). Rain-day chorus: multiple males in overlapping territories singing simultaneously on storm days.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Some local variation in specific note repertoires. Pacific Wren (T. pacificus) song is consistently more complex than Winter Wren (T. hiemalis) -- the bioacoustic difference that contributed to the 2010 species split.
song_metrics.key_property: 113+ NOTES PER SONG -- the most complex birdsong of any North American bird. Rain-day chorus in wet old-growth. BIOLOGY RETURNS at degree 268 after 23 consecutive abiotic degrees (245-267). RETURN from degree 001 (267-degree gap). First species in SPS catalog returns near the deepest point of the catalog. E=5 but information-dense: the most note-dense five in the engine. ~16 notes/second. Wide frequency range 2,500-9,000+ Hz. Small bird (8-12g), maximum acoustic complexity.

plumage.overall: Very small passerine. Rich brown above with fine barring on wings, tail, and flanks. Buffy-white eyebrow stripe (supercilium). Pale underparts with fine barring on flanks and belly. Short, stubby bill. Short tail habitually cocked upward -- often perpendicular to the body. No sexual dimorphism in plumage.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: For degree 268: tiny brown bird with short cocked tail in wet old-growth understory producing an impossibly complex cascading song during rain. The song is the field mark. Nothing else in the PNW forest sounds like this.
plumage.flight_pattern: Low, rapid, whirring flight through dense understory. Rarely flies high or in open. Stays within the low structural complexity of the forest floor.

physical_description.overall: Very small passerine. Body length 9-11 cm. Weight 8-12 grams. One of North America's smallest songbirds. Smaller than a House Sparrow. Rich brown with fine barring, buffy supercilium, cocked short tail.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 268: tiny brown bird with cocked tail in wet old-growth forest, singing during rain, producing cascading ultra-complex song. If you are in wet PNW old-growth on a rainy day and hear an impossible cascade of notes from low in the vegetation -- Pacific Wren.
physical_description.field_id: Tiny. Brown. Cocked tail. Wet old-growth. Rain day. The song identifies it before you see it.

nodes.title: Biology Returns -- 23 Abiotic Degrees End
nodes.description: Last biological entry at degree 244 (White Sturgeon). Pacific Wren at 268 ends the 23-degree abiotic arc (245-267). The most complex birdsong in North America re-enters the engine after the most overwhelming geophysical event in the arc. Maximum contrast.

nodes.title: 113+ Notes Per Song -- Most Complex in North America
nodes.description: 113+ distinct note types per continuous song bout. ~16 notes per second. 5-10 second duration. Wide frequency range 2,500-9,000+ Hz. Most note-dense song of any North American bird. Sexual selection: song complexity honestly signals cognitive quality.

nodes.title: Rain-Day Chorus -- Counterintuitive Singing Behavior
nodes.description: Pacific Wrens sing MORE in rain. Storm day: multiple males singing simultaneously in overlapping territories. Understory acoustic pocket during rain. Amplified, layered, multi-voice chorus. One of the most remarkable natural acoustic events in PNW old-growth.

nodes.title: Returns from Degree 001 -- 267-Degree Gap
nodes.description: Pacific Wren was the first SPS species entry. Returns at 268 in rain-day chorus context. 267-degree gap. The engine's first species re-enters near the deepest point of the catalog. The circle begins to close.

nodes.title: Troglodytidae -- Cave-Dwellers of the Forest Floor
nodes.description: Troglodytes (Greek: cave-dweller). Forages in dark interior of log tangles, root masses, fern curtains. 2010 split from Winter Wren. Second Troglodytidae entry after Bewick's Wren (SPS-055). Family defined by song complexity.

cross_references.relationship: RETURN! First entry in entire SPS catalog. Returns at 268 in rain-day chorus context. 267-degree gap. Degree 001 documented the species; degree 268 isolates the rain-day chorus.

MAXIMUM CONTRAST. E=8 deep rumbling wall movement (abiotic, overwhelming) → E=5 Pacific Wren rain-day chorus (biological, intricate). One-degree transition. The largest energy and character contrast between adjacent entries.
Last biological entry before 23-degree abiotic arc. White Sturgeon at 244; Pacific Wren at 268. The 23 abiotic degrees between these two living creatures.
Troglodytidae family. Bewick's Wren in shrubby edge habitat. Pacific Wren in wet old-growth interior. Two wren entries representing the family's PNW habitat range.
The rain the Pacific Wren sings in. Abiotic rain at 248; biological response to rain at 268. Same hydrological context, two acoustic registers.
